current_fact.fact_text: In 1954, U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower authorized the creation of the United States Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs. Built high on the Rocky Mountain front, the academy became a blend of futuristic architecture, military discipline, and aerodynamic science dedicated to training generations of pilots and engineers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

current_fact.match_reason: An academy devoted to training pilots and leaders fits an episode about learning, discipline, and the emotional arc of mastering difficult skills.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 2001, a U.S. Navy EP-3E surveillance plane collided mid-air with a Chinese J-8 fighter over the South China Sea. The American crew executed an emergency landing on Hainan Island and were detained for 11 days, turning a split-second navigational incident into a major diplomatic standoff.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A tense reconnaissance mission turning into an unexpected forced landing echoes the idea of ‘drifting beyond the expected’ and suddenly changing course, like the freeform Evening Flow mixes.

queue.fact_text: In 1948, the Faroe Islands gained home rule within the Kingdom of Denmark, becoming an autonomous territory with their own parliament. This remote North Atlantic archipelago, known for dramatic cliffs and seabird colonies, used political compromise to preserve its language, culture, and control over local affairs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: Island autonomy in the North Atlantic parallels an episode voyaging between Mediterranean shores; both celebrate coastal cultures shaping their own identity.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 1944, American bombers accidentally struck the neutral Swiss city of Schaffhausen after navigation errors led them off-course from Germany. The raid killed dozens of civilians and forced the United States to issue apologies and pay reparations, highlighting how fragile neutrality can be from the air.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

queue.match_reason: A tragic bombing caused by mistaken assumptions echoes an episode about questioning what we think we know and the consequences when we don’t ‘think again.’

queue.fact_text: In 1945, as World War II neared its end, the U.S. Tenth Army launched an assault on Japan’s Thirty-Second Army on Okinawa. The battle became one of the bloodiest in the Pacific, with ferocious fighting, civilian casualties, and kamikaze attacks that foreshadowed the human cost of any invasion of mainland Japan.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A brutal, decisive battle in the Pacific War pairs with an episode about political rage and anti-war resistance, reflecting on the cost of militarized power.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 1939, Generalísimo Francisco Franco declared the end of the Spanish Civil War, after the last Republican forces surrendered. The brutal conflict, which had drawn in foreign volunteers and fascist powers, gave way to decades of authoritarian rule known as Francoist Spain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The end of the Spanish Civil War and the beginning of Franco’s regime profoundly shaped Spanish culture; an episode on flamenco’s history lets us hear that tension and resilience in song.

queue.fact_text: In 1989, the UK government introduced the Community Charge in Scotland, better known as the "poll tax." This flat-rate local tax, widely criticized as unfair, sparked mass protests and civil disobedience that helped erode support for Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A controversial tax hitting ordinary people fits with Sheffield’s sharp musical storytelling about class, ‘common people,’ and life under shifting British politics.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 1970, U.S. President Richard Nixon signed the Public Health Cigarette Smoking Act, banning cigarette advertising on television and radio and strengthening health warnings. It marked a rare moment when the airwaves themselves were cleared to protect public health over commercial interests.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A law reshaping what could be broadcast over radio and TV parallels an episode about a technology that quietly reshaped how voices sound on those same airwaves.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 2011, protests in Mazar-i-Sharif, Afghanistan, over a Florida pastor’s Quran burning escalated into a violent attack on a United Nations compound. Fourteen people, including seven UN workers, were killed, exposing how a symbolic act on one continent can ignite deadly fury on another.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A deadly chain reaction from one provocative act to global outrage reflects an episode about political critique, responsibility, and the power of words crossing borders.

queue.fact_text: In 285, Roman Emperor Diocletian elevated Maximian to co-emperor as "Augustus," effectively founding the Tetrarchy system that would split imperial power. This experiment in shared rule was meant to stabilize a sprawling empire by dividing responsibilities—and the aura of absolute authority.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: Diocletian sharing imperial power with a trusted partner echoes an episode built on shared voices and co-hosted storytelling, turning leadership into something collaborative.

queue.fact_text: On April 1, 1969, the same day the Harrier was entering service, another sort of revolution was underway: the sound barrier in pop culture was starting to bend, with music and media crossing borders faster than ever via radio and TV. That era’s experiments form the background radiation of much of today’s global music landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A year of musical upheaval and cross-border experimentation matches an episode about Afrobeat’s rise—a genre literally built from collisions between local rhythms and global influences.

queue.fact_text: By 2001, the EP-3E incident highlighted how surveillance technology had turned the sky into a contested data battlefield. Signals intelligence aircraft like the EP-3E quietly listen to radar, radio, and electronic whispers, mapping the invisible conversations that modern militaries rely on.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Aircraft designed to intercept invisible signals resonate with an episode about our intimate, invisible data streams through phones and the psychological cost of constant connection.

queue.fact_text: Throughout the late 20th century, marine disasters like the SS Atlantic’s and later oil spills pushed scientists to study ocean currents and coastal ecology with new urgency. These tragedies inadvertently advanced our understanding of how energy and life move through the world’s oceans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: Ocean science born from catastrophe pairs with an episode themed around immersion, water, and going below the surface—turning loss into deeper understanding.

queue.fact_text: By the early 21st century, comets like Hale–Bopp and satellite images from missions like TIROS had shifted how humans see Earth: not as an endless stage, but as a small, glowing sphere in a dark sea. This ‘overview effect’—seeing our world from outside—has quietly influenced art, activism, and even how we tell stories about home.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: An expanded planetary perspective—the overview effect—belongs with a cosmic, journey-themed episode that literally invites listeners toward a distant destination: Sedna.

published.fact_text: On April 1, 2026, Artemis II became the first crewed deep-space mission since Apollo 17 in 1972, sending humans once again on a loop around the Moon. The mission tests NASA’s new Orion spacecraft and Space Launch System, rehearsing for future lunar landings and long-duration journeys deeper into the Solar System.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal leap back into deep space fits perfectly with an episode built around reimagining ‘space’ as possibility and forward motion.

published.fact_text: In 1960, the TIROS-1 satellite transmitted the first television pictures from space, showing swirling cloud systems over Earth. These grainy black-and-white images quietly revolutionized weather forecasting, proving that orbiting cameras could watch our planet’s atmosphere in motion.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The first TV signal from orbit is a literal ‘electric shock’ from space, mirroring the episode’s charged, high-voltage rock energy.

published.fact_text: On April 1, 1997, Comet Hale–Bopp swept through perihelion, becoming one of the brightest and longest-observed comets of the 20th century. Visible to the naked eye for an astonishing 18 months, it captivated skywatchers worldwide and even inspired cults, art, and scientific studies of primordial Solar System material.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A great comet glowing in the cold night sky pairs with an Arctic, cinematic episode; both evoke awe in vast, blue-black spaces filled with rare visitors.

published.fact_text: In 1973, India launched Project Tiger at Jim Corbett National Park, creating a network of reserves to save tigers from the brink of extinction. At a time when the country’s tiger population had plunged below 2,000, this ambitious experiment in large-scale conservation became a global model for protecting apex predators and their ecosystems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode center on survival in wild landscapes and the fight to preserve ways of life—tigers in Indian forests, Tuareg culture in the desert.

published.fact_text: The 1946 Aleutian Islands earthquake, with a magnitude of 8.6, triggered a devastating tsunami that crossed the Pacific and struck Hawaii. In Hilo, waves up to 13–18 meters high destroyed entire neighborhoods, revealing how seafloor quakes in remote arcs can reshape distant coastlines overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A catastrophic ocean surge and an episode about storms, rain, and resilience both explore how overwhelming forces eventually give way to cautious hope.

published.fact_text: In 1873, the SS Atlantic, a White Star Line steamer, struck rocks off Nova Scotia and sank in the night, killing 547 people. It was the worst transatlantic maritime disaster before the Titanic, and the tragedy reshaped safety practices for passenger liners on the North Atlantic routes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A historic shipwreck off cold Atlantic shores contrasts with, and gives depth to, a modern, sunlit sailing episode—all about respect for the sea’s power.

published.fact_text: On Easter Sunday 1725, Johann Sebastian Bach’s early version of what became his Easter Oratorio was first performed at St. Nicholas Church in Leipzig. The piece recycled and reshaped earlier secular music, showing how Bach quietly transformed everyday material into something liturgical and luminous.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A baroque Easter oratorio and a contemporary jazz meditation both turn structured composition into a kind of spiritual calm, ideal for early-morning reflection.

published.fact_text: In 1984, Marvin Gaye was shot and killed by his father in their Los Angeles home, one day before his 45th birthday. The soul icon behind “What’s Going On” and “Sexual Healing” left a complicated legacy of musical genius interwoven with family conflict, mental health struggles, and activism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Remembering a soul legend’s tragic death sits naturally next to an episode honoring powerful vocalists; both spotlight how vulnerable, human voices can become immortal.

published.fact_text: On April 1, 1976, Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak officially founded Apple Computer, Inc. in a California garage. Their small experiment in personal computing grew into a company that would reshape design, music listening, communication, and even how we carry entire worlds in our pockets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Apple’s birth launched the era of personal devices; an episode about our tangled relationship with smartphones is a direct emotional sequel to that 1976 garage decision.

published.fact_text: In 1969, the Hawker Siddeley Harrier entered service with the Royal Air Force as the world’s first operational fighter capable of vertical/short takeoff and landing. Its swiveling jet nozzles allowed it to hover, rise, and dart like a mechanical hummingbird—rewriting what a combat aircraft could do near the ground.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The Harrier’s radical ‘lift-off’ mirrors Sedna FM’s own first explosive launch episode, both pioneering new ways to move through their respective skies.

published.fact_text: In 1954, U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower authorized the creation of the United States Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs. Built high on the Rocky Mountain front, the academy became a blend of futuristic architecture, military discipline, and aerodynamic science dedicated to training generations of pilots and engineers.

episode.description:

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.
